column1: Here is a comprehensive timeline table integrating the evolution of Yemo/Yama and their variants, the development of Satan/Ahriman/Devil, the role of chthonic (underworld) deities across Indo-European cultures, and the etymological roots and connections (or lack thereof) between Yama/Yemo and YHVH/Yahweh/Jehovah. This table also highlights the PIE (Proto-Indo-European) links between various underworld deities and motifs.

column3: Yemo/Yama & Variants Development
column4: Chthonic/Underworld Deities & Motifs
column5: Devil/Satan/Ahriman Development

column3: Yemo: Primordial twin, slain by Manu; his body becomes the world.
column4: Dʰéǵʰōm: Earth-goddess, abode of the dead; underworld as the final dwelling of mortals.
column5: No devil concept.
column6: No Yahweh cult; Northwest Semitic deities (El, Baal, etc.)
column7: Yemo: PIE root yem- (“twin”). Dʰéǵʰōm: root for earth/underworld. No etymological link to YHVH.

column3: Yama (India): First mortal, ruler of the dead. Yima (Iran): King, ruler of paradise.
column4: Yama rules the land of the dead; Vala (Vedic), Veles (Slavic) as underworld/earth gods.
column5: No personified evil; dualism not yet developed.
column6: No Yahweh cult in Indo-Iranian world.
column7: Yama: Sanskrit from PIE yem-. Veles: Slavic, possibly from PIE Welnos, linked to earth/underworld.

column3: No Yama; Canaanite death gods: Mot, Resheph.
column4: Mot: God of death and underworld; chthonic sacrifices.
column5: No devil concept.
column6: Yahweh: Appears as a storm/war god in Canaanite pantheon.
column7: No etymological overlap; YHVH possibly from h-y-h (“to be”).

column3: Yama: God of death, judge of souls, rides buffalo.
column4: Yama as king of the dead; Nirrti (goddess of decay); Ratri (night).
column7: Yama and YHVH: No linguistic or mythological connection.

column3: Yima: First king, ruler of paradise.
column4: Angra Mainyu/Ahriman: Underworld adversary; Druj (deceit, chaos).
column5: Ahriman: Principle of evil, dualistic adversary.
column7: Ahriman: Not PIE, but dualistic evil develops.

column3: No Yama; Yahweh rules a pantheon; disasters from Yahweh or lesser gods.
column4: Rephaim: Spirits of the dead; Sheol as underworld.
column5: No Satan as adversary; angels unnamed.
column6: Yahweh: Head of Israelite pantheon; polytheistic context.
column7: YHVH: Tetragrammaton, likely from h-y-h (“to be”); no Indo-European link.

column5: No devil concept.
column6: Hezekiah/Josiah reforms: move toward Yahweh alone (monolatry).

column5: No Satan as adversary; suffering attributed to Yahweh.
column6: Collapse of pantheon; Yahweh as sole god.

column5: Satan emerges as heavenly being (Zechariah, Job), not yet opponent.
column7: No etymological overlap; Yama myth not present in Israelite tradition.

column5: Satan/Devil personified as cosmic evil; stories of fallen angels (Azazel, 1 Enoch).

column3: Yama: Judge of dead; Yamantaka: "Yama-slayer," wrathful deity.
column4: Yama as judge of the dead; Bhairava as fierce guardian.

column3: Yanluo Wang (China), Enma (Japan): Yama as underworld judge.
column4: Yanluo Wang: Judge of the dead; Diyu (Chinese hell).
column7: Yama imported via Buddhism; no YHVH connection.

column4: Hades: Ruler of the underworld; Persephone: Queen; Charon: Ferryman; Thanatos: Death; Erinyes: Furies; Hecate: Magic/necromancy; Judges: Minos, Rhadamanthys, Aiakos; Nyx: Night; Tartarus: Abyss; Moirae: Fates; Erebus: Darkness; Palici: Twin chthonic gods.
column7: Hades: Possibly Indo-European, parallels with Veles and Yama; chthonic gods linked to PIE earth/underworld goddess Dʰéǵʰōm.

column4: Veles: God of earth, waters, underworld, magic, cattle, music; opponent of Perun; associated with Nav (underworld).
column7: Veles: Possibly from PIE Welnos, parallels to Hades, Hermes, Odin, Yama.

column5: Satan/Devil becomes central adversary in Christian theology.

column3: Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava): Wrathful, buffalo-headed, multi-armed; subdues Yama.
column4: Yamantaka as "conqueror of death," many forms.

column5: Devil/Satan entrenched in theology; St. Christopher as monstrous/dog-headed threshold guardian (Yama-like motif).
column7: St. Christopher: Guardian/psychopomp motif, parallels to Anubis, Yama, Veles.

column3: Religious Beliefs & Practices
column4: Key Events, Conflicts, and Schisms
column5: Sources

column3: Polytheistic: El, Baal, Asherah, etc.
column4: Canaanite city-states dominate; religious syncretism common

column3: Early Yahweh worship possibly emerges; shared Canaanite deities and customs
column4: Proto-Israelites emerge within Canaanite society

column3: Yahweh worship present; Canaanite deities persist
column4: "Israel" mentioned on Merneptah Stele; Israelites appear in Canaan

column3: Yahweh worship spreads; Asherah, Baal, and others still venerated
column4: Archaeology: new highland villages with Israelite identity

column3: Yahweh as national god, but polytheistic practices endure
column4: Formation of Israelite state; syncretism and idol worship reported in biblical texts

column3: Official Yahwism, but Asherah and Baal worship widespread
column4: Prophetic and royal opposition to "idolatry"; Asherah worship in temples; Elijah vs. prophets of Baal and Asherah

column3: Yahweh’s name attested outside Israel
column4: Moabite Stone (Mesha Stele) contains earliest known extra-biblical reference to Yahweh

column3: Moves toward monolatry/monotheism, but popular religion includes Asherah
column4: Assyrian conquest of Israel (722 BCE); reforms in Judah begin

column3: Josiah’s reforms: centralization of Yahweh worship, purging of Asherah and Baal
column4: Josiah removes Asherah from Temple, burns symbols, suppresses goddess worship

column3: Monotheism solidifies among elite; popular religion still varied
column4: Babylonian conquest; Temple destroyed; exile accelerates monotheistic Yahwism

column3: Monotheism institutionalized; Asherah/Baal worship marginalized
column4: Return from exile; Second Temple built; official suppression of earlier syncretism

column3: Yahweh becomes sole deity; Judaism revised and canonized
column4: Yahweh as state god of Judah; monotheism established; avoidance of pronouncing the name

column3: Name YHWH (Tetragrammaton) not pronounced; "Adonai" or "Elohim" substituted
column4: Greek and Latin translations use "Kyrios" and "Dominus"; "the LORD" in English Bibles

column3: Vowel points of "Adonai" or "Elohim" added to YHWH, creating "Yehovah"/"Jehovah" in later Christian use
column4: Jews avoid pronouncing YHWH; Christian scholars Latinize the name as "Jehovah"
